# Office Display Manager — Overview

<!-- Generated by scripts/generate-docs.mjs on `npm run build`. Do not edit generated
     pages by hand — change the source and rerun. Authored prose (Overview / Architecture
     notes on the Overview page) lives in BEGIN:manual / END:manual blocks and is preserved. -->

<!-- BEGIN:manual:overview -->
## Overview

Office Display Manager (ODM) drives office screens (TVs, kiosks) as unattended
slideshows of web URLs. A manager creates a **Slideshow** record, lists the URLs
to rotate, assigns it to a **service account** (the technical user a screen logs
in as), and sets a slide duration and working-hours window. The screen opens a
single permanent URL and plays whatever active slideshow is assigned to its
account — rotating slides in a sandboxed iframe, blanking to a standby clock
outside working hours, and showing an idle card when nothing is assigned.

The whole app is one custom table plus a self-contained HTML player served two
ways: a Scripted REST API and a direct UI page. There are **no custom roles** —
access is ownership-based (creator / assigned service account / platform admin).

> The sections below the app-fact table are **generated** from source by
> `scripts/generate-docs.mjs`. Edit prose only inside the `manual` blocks.
<!-- END:manual:overview -->

<!-- BEGIN:manual:architecture -->
## Architecture notes

**Request paths.** A screen can reach the player two ways, both session-authenticated:

- **Direct UI page** `x_804244_odm_player.do?screen=<user_name>` — `direct: true`,
  so the platform adds no UI16/Polaris chrome. A Jelly page calls the
  `OdmPlayerPage` script include, which `require()`s the server module and returns
  the fully rendered player document (deck + session token injected server-side).
  This is the address-bar entry point; anonymous visitors get the login redirect
  for free.
- **Scripted REST API** under `/api/x_804244_odm/player` — serves the same HTML
  plus a JSON `deck` route the player polls for live updates. REST rejects
  cookie-only calls (X-UserToken CSRF rule), which is why the deck carries a
  session token for the player's `fetch` polling.

**Layering.** Pure logic (`src/server/deck.ts`, `src/client/templates/player.js`)
has no Glide or DOM dependencies, so Jest covers every branch. The thin Glide
layer (`handlers.src.ts`) does record access and enforces the open-time access
rule. `scripts/build-template.mjs` inlines `player.js` into `rotator.html` and
concatenates the pure + handler sources into the self-contained, committed
`src/server/player-routes.ts` — the only module the SDK actually deploys.

**Security model.** Table ACLs gate CRUD by ownership (see the ACL table). The
player's *open-time* check is separate and lives in the handler: a slideshow
opens only for its creator, its assigned service account, a platform admin, or
any slideshow flagged `public`. The record is read with a plain `GlideRecord`
(not `GlideRecordSecure`) precisely so the handler can distinguish "no slideshow"
(idle) from "exists but forbidden" (access-denied). Manager-supplied content is
`<`/`>`-escaped before injection so it can never break out of the
CDATA-wrapped script context.

**Invariants.** At most one active slideshow per service account (enforced by the
`unique active slideshow` business rule) so the player always resolves exactly
one deck per screen. `player_link` is system-maintained by a business rule and
kept read-only on the form by a UI policy.
<!-- END:manual:architecture -->
